checks.a_analysis: I compared each paragraph and heading in the JSON output against the provided source PDF. All text content in the JSON corresponds directly to text found in the source document. There are no invented sections or sentences.

I verified all contact information including phone numbers (800-722-3946, 1-800-858-0368), email addresses (NCEOC@ncdps.gov, ncisaac@ncsbi.gov, DITThreatManagement@nc.gov), and URLs (https://it.nc.gov/report). Every piece of contact information in the JSON accurately matches the source PDF.

The source document consists of 3 pages. I confirmed that all sections from these pages (e.g., 'When to Report to the State', 'What to Report', 'How to Report Cyber Incidents to the State', 'Types of State Incident Response', 'Mission-Critical Support') are present in the JSON output. No pages were dropped.

The table 'Reporting Points of Contact' on page 2 of the PDF contains all the relevant data points in the JSON. The JSON structure represents the headers and the unequal number of items in the columns correctly by including a final row for the last agency contact which had no corresponding item in the second column.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I conducted a line-by-line comparison of major sections like the CSTF description and the Incident Response activities. All descriptive text and bullet points have been preserved faithfully.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The JSON preserves the meaning of the source document throughout. Minor reformatting such as combining two short paragraphs on page 3 or changing the visual placement of 'Note:' does not alter the information provided to the user.

checks.a_analysis: The output document was reviewed for duplicated text blocks. No instances were found where the same paragraph, list, or section was repeated. The transition between pages correctly handles the content without duplicating the text that spans the page breaks.

The document starts with an H1 for the title, followed by an H2 subtitle. Subsequent sections use H3 and H2. While the transition from H3 (index 9) back to H2 (index 19) is a decrease in level, it represents a change in major section. No levels are skipped in an upward direction (e.g., H1 to H3), which is the primary concern for screen reader navigation.

The table 'Reporting Points of Contact' (Index 25) contains several structural errors. First, the column headers ('State Agencies' and 'Local Governments...') in the second row (Index 25, Row 1) are marked as 'data' cells rather than 'header' cells. Second, and more critically, the final row (Row 4) contains only a single cell. Because the table is established as having two columns in preceding rows, a row with only one cell and no 'colspan' property corrupts the table grid, making the content associated with that row semantically orphaned from the column structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table on page 2 has two distinct columns. The final row has content in the left column ('Contact the Enterprise Security...') and an empty space in the right column.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the table at element index 25, row 4 (is_header_row: false) contains only one cell object: 'Contact the Enterprise Security and Risk Management Office at mailto:DITThreatManagement@nc.gov.'
checks.b_evidence_location: How to Report Cyber Incidents to the State, Page 2, element index 25

checks.a_analysis: The reading order is disrupted by the injection of page header images into the middle of a list. In the source, a list regarding cyber incident criteria starts at the bottom of page 1 and concludes at the top of page 2. The logos (header) for page 2 appear at the very top of the page. However, the output places these images (Index 13, 14, 15) between the first part of the list (Index 12) and the second part (Index 16). This forces a user to read through irrelevant header/logo metadata in the middle of a logical list of criteria, breaking the flow of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The list of incident criteria begins on Page 1 ('Result in a significant loss...') and continues seamlessly onto Page 2 ('Indicate unauthorized access...'). Logos are in the header area.
checks.b_evidence_output: The images (Logos) at indices 13, 14, and 15 are positioned in the reading order between list items at index 12 and index 16.
checks.b_evidence_location: When to Report to the State, between Page 1 and Page 2

checks.a_analysis: A single continuous list in the source has been split into two separate list objects (Index 12 and Index 16) due to the intervention of the page header content. This changes the meaning of the document, as the list at Index 16 appears as a new, unrelated list rather than a continuation of the requirements specified in the paragraph at Index 11.
checks.b_evidence_source: A single list of 5 bullets spanning the page break between page 1 and page 2.
checks.b_evidence_output: Two separate list elements at index 12 (2 items) and index 16 (3 items).
checks.b_evidence_location: When to Report to the State, Page 1 and 2

checks.a_analysis: The output contains all URLs and email addresses present in the source. While they are rendered as plain text within the paragraphs and table cells rather than semantic anchor tags, the data is complete and accurate with no hallucinations.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the document is significantly disrupted. The header logos (NCDIT, DPS, and the Great Seal) which appear at the very top of the source PDF have been moved and inserted into the middle of the document content. Specifically, they are placed between the second and third bullet points of the list under the 'When to Report to the State' section. This breaks the logical flow of the list and the overall document structure, making it feel disjointed and misorganized.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF has logos at the top of Page 1. A continuous bulleted list begins on Page 1 ('Result in a significant loss of data...') and continues on Page 2 ('Indicate unauthorized access to...').
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML, the three logos (DPS, NCDIT, Great Seal) are inserted between the list item 'Have an impact on a large number of victims' and the list item 'Indicate unauthorized access to...'.
checks.b_evidence_location: In the 'When to Report to the State' section, between the second and third bullet points.

checks.a_analysis: The table 'Reporting Points of Contact' is correctly rendered in the HTML. It maintains its two-column structure and headers. While the styling (colors) differs from the original, the data remains organized in a grid format that is easily readable and understandable by a sighted user.
All meaningful images from the source PDF (the NCDIT logo, the DPS logo, and the Great Seal of North Carolina) are present in the HTML output. None of the images appear to be broken or missing.

There is no visual evidence of duplicated body content (paragraphs, lists, or tables) within the HTML rendering. The section headings appearing in the Table of Contents and again in the main body is expected accessible design behavior and not a defect.
